kjv_text: I will declare thy name unto my brethren: in the midst of the congregation will I praise thee.

thread.thread: Psalm 22 does not end at the cross — at verse 22 the forsaken sufferer turns from anguish to praise, standing alive in the midst of the congregation. Hebrews 2:12 takes that very opening line and puts it in the mouth of the RISEN Christ, and Hebrews 2:11 says He is not ashamed to call them brethren. Cross -> resurrection -> family: the risen Christ calls you in.
thread.lever: NT-confirmed OT echo (Hebrews 2:12 cites Ps 22:22 of the risen Christ; resurrection NT-warranted)
thread.anchor_detail: the risen Christ alive on the far side of the cross, declaring the Father's name among His brethren, gathering a family
thread.why_fresh: the same psalm that records the forsaken cry turns at verse 22 to a living, praising voice — and Hebrews names that voice as the risen Jesus, not ashamed to call us brethren
thread.gospel_landing: the same Jesus who cried out forsaken now lives; the risen Christ is not ashamed to call His own brethren and is calling you into that family; come to Him
thread.rationale: the airtight Hebrews citation that completes the cross->resurrection arc, with a grace landing that brings the listener into the family

beats.text: Most people stop reading Psalm twenty-two at the cross. Keep going — it doesn't end in a grave.

But the cry doesn't stay forsaken. Later, the psalm turns — the same voice moves from anguish to praise: "I will declare thy name unto my brethren: in the midst of the congregation will I praise thee."
Who is that praising voice? Hebrews takes that opening line — word for word — and puts it in the mouth of the risen Christ. The psalm's turn is Jesus, alive on the far side of the cross.
And hear what He calls us. Hebrews says He is — "not ashamed to call them brethren" — brothers. Family.
The same Jesus who cried out forsaken now lives — and the risen Christ is not ashamed to call His own brethren. He is calling you into that family.
